title: Inline-vs-pointer discrimination for TIFF value fields is duplicated across multiple parse functions
description: The logic for deciding whether a TIFF entry's value is inline (count * sizeof(type) <= 4) or a file pointer is scattered across `IfdEntry::as_u32`, `parse_olympus_makernote` (the `inline_u16` closure), `parse_image_processing_subifd`, and the camera-settings parser. Each site makes slightly different decisions about which counts are inline (e.g. `cnt <= 2` vs `cnt == 2` vs `cnt == 1`), creating inconsistencies and potential future bugs. A single `fn tiff_value_bytes(entry, reader) -> Result<SmallVec<[u8;4]>>` or similar helper that centralises the inline/pointer dispatch would eliminate the duplication and the discrepancy in the SHORT[2] case found in issue `logic-a3b4c5d6`.
evidence: Verified in tiff.rs: `as_u32` (line 237) uses count==1 for inline SHORT; the `inline_u16` closure (line 346) uses cnt<=2; `parse_image_processing_subifd` (line 511) uses cnt==2 for inline; `parse_camera_settings_subifd` (line 484) always reads inline. The inconsistency is real and structural — four distinct dispatch patterns with no shared utility.

title: separable_blur allocates two full-image buffers and has an inner kernel loop that defeats LLVM auto-vectorization
description: For a 20MP image with the 13-tap clarity kernel, separable_blur executes ~780M multiply-adds across two passes (H-pass then V-pass), each touching width×height×3×13 iterations. The innermost loop iterates over the kernel with a per-iteration clamped index calculation `(x as isize + ki as isize - half as isize).clamp(...)` that introduces a data-dependent address computation per tap, preventing LLVM from emitting SIMD gather/load sequences. Two `vec![0u16; n]` allocations of ~120MB each (for a 20MP image: 5472×3648×3×2 bytes = ~120MB) occur on every blur call. When both texture and clarity are active, four such allocations happen back-to-back.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 173-207 match exactly: `temp = vec![0u16; n]` and `out = vec![0u16; n]` are both allocated on every call, and the inner kernel loop uses a clamp-based isize index computation per tap (line 183-185) that prevents SIMD vectorization. Two large heap allocations per `separable_blur` call is confirmed.

title: process() rebuilds three 65536-entry pre-LUTs and one 65536-entry post-LUT on every apply_look call, totalling ~384KB of computation at interactive slider rates
description: Each call to `process()` invokes `build_pre_lut` three times and `build_post_lut` once. Each LUT iterates 65536 entries: the pre-LUT does two float multiplies and a clamp per entry; the post-LUT calls `tone_curve` which contains up to three `powf` calls (shadows gamma, highlights gamma, sRGB EOTF). At 60fps drag on a lightbox image, `apply_look` calls `process` on every animation frame, re-evaluating all four LUTs each time regardless of which slider moved. The powf calls inside `build_post_lut` are especially costly: at 65536 iterations with potential 3 powf each, that is up to ~196K `powf` calls per frame.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 241-252: `build_pre_lut` is called three times and `build_post_lut` once unconditionally in every `process()` call. `build_post_lut` (line 154-161) iterates 65536 entries calling `tone_curve`, which calls `powf` up to 3 times per entry (lines 115, 123, 81). No caching or param-change detection exists; every `apply_look` call triggers a full rebuild.

title: apply_unsharp_masks calls separable_blur twice, allocating and immediately discarding up to four full-image buffers (~480MB total for 20MP)
description: When both texture and clarity are non-zero, `apply_unsharp_masks` calls `apply_unsharp_mask` twice, each of which calls `separable_blur`, which itself allocates two `Vec<u16>` of `width * height * 3` elements. For a 20MP image that is 4 × 120MB = ~480MB of allocation and deallocation per re-render triggered by slider drag. These buffers cannot be reused across the two calls because each has different dimensions (same here, since full-image) but the blur intermediates are discarded after the sharpening blend. The allocations also stress WASM's linear memory, which cannot release pages back to the OS.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 220-228 and 209-218 match exactly. When both texture and clarity are nonzero, `apply_unsharp_mask` is called twice, each calling `separable_blur` which allocates two full-image `Vec<u16>` buffers. The allocation pattern is confirmed; the WASM linear memory concern is accurate and real.

title: Channel-interleaved loop order in separable_blur prevents auto-vectorization across channels
description: The innermost loop structure is `y → x → c → ki`, where `c` iterates over 3 channels inside the pixel loop. This interleaving means each channel's accumulation is mixed with index computations for the other channels, preventing LLVM from widening across the 3-channel dimension. Reordering to `c → y → x → ki` (with planar intermediate buffers) would allow the `x` inner loop to be a contiguous SIMD-friendly reduction. With WASM SIMD (128-bit, 4 f32 lanes), the kernel accumulation loop over 5 or 13 taps could be vectorized across 4 output pixels at once, giving a theoretical 4x speedup on the inner loop.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 178-190 match exactly: loop order is y→x→c→ki with interleaved channel writes into an RGB-interleaved buffer. The stride-3 access pattern (`src[(y*width+xi)*3+c]`) in the innermost loop makes auto-vectorization across channels impossible. This is a real structural vectorization blocker.

title: downscale_rgb16_impl recomputes floating-point row/column bounds (x0, x1, y0, y1) per output pixel using f32 arithmetic that is not hoisted out of the column loop
description: In `downscale_rgb16_impl`, the vertical span `y0` and `y1` depends only on `dy` and `yr`, so it could be computed once per row. However, `x0` and `x1` recompute `dx as f32 * xr` and `((dx as f32) + 1.0) * xr` per pixel. For a 5472×3648 → 1800×1200 downscale, the outer loop runs 1200 times and the inner loop 1800 times, performing 3600 f32 multiplies for bounds that could be integer-incremented. The `y0..y1` and `x0..x1` inner loops also recompute the flat index `y * sw + x` without hoisting the `y * sw` stride, causing an extra multiply per source pixel in the box.
evidence: lib.rs lines 148-165 confirm: y0/y1 are correctly hoisted to the outer dy loop (lines 149-151), but x0/x1 are recomputed per dx (lines 153-155), and the inner loop at line 160 computes `(y * sw + x) * 3` without hoisting `y * sw`. The description is accurate. Severity is genuinely low (LLVM strength-reduction likely handles the multiply), but the redundancy is real.

title: apply_look unpacks rgb16 bytes into a Vec<u16> using a per-element map/collect, missing a safe bytemuck or copy_from_slice path
description: The `apply_look` function unpacks `rgb16_bytes` (6 bytes per pixel, packed u16 LE) into `Vec<u16>` using `(0..n).map(|i| u16::from_le_bytes([rgb16_bytes[i*2], rgb16_bytes[i*2+1]])).collect()`. This generates an iterator that accesses two non-adjacent bytes per step and builds a Vec element-by-element, with bounds checks on each pair. A `bytemuck::cast_slice` (zero-copy reinterpret on LE platforms, which WASM is) or a `u16::from_le_bytes` loop with `chunks_exact(2)` would be more efficient and still safe. For a 1800×1200 lightbox buffer that is 2.16M elements being individually converted on every slider drag.
evidence: lib.rs lines 430-433 match exactly. The index-based map/collect pattern with `i*2` and `i*2+1` is confirmed; LLVM can auto-vectorize `chunks_exact(2)` more reliably than the index arithmetic form. The `bytemuck` approach would require a dependency but `chunks_exact(2)` is stdlib. Real and actionable, though the actual performance gap is likely small since LLVM will often optimize this.

title: rgb_to_rgba pushes one byte at a time rather than using extend_from_slice or unsafe ptr copy
description: The `rgb_to_rgba` function pushes 4 bytes per pixel individually into a pre-capacity Vec. While LLVM will likely vectorize 4 sequential `push` calls on a Vec with spare capacity, the explicit `push` API still goes through the Vec growth boundary check on each call. Using `out.extend_from_slice(&[r, g, b, 255])` or building the array inline and writing 4 bytes at once via a `chunks_mut(4)` approach is cleaner and more reliably vectorized. For a 20MP image this is 20M pixels × 4 pushes = 80M individual calls through the Vec::push code path.
evidence: lib.rs lines 474-484 match exactly. The Vec is pre-allocated with `with_capacity(n*4)` so no reallocation occurs, and LLVM is likely to optimize consecutive bounded pushes into a single 4-byte store, but `extend_from_slice(&[r,g,b,255])` is a cleaner and more explicitly vectorizable pattern. Real finding at low severity; the claim about '80M individual Vec::push code path calls' is somewhat overstated since LLVM inlines and vectorizes, but the improvement opportunity is real.

title: process() inner pixel loop computes luma and vibrance weighting with floating-point per pixel even when vibrance is zero
description: For every pixel, `process` computes `luma`, `raw_mx`, `mx`, `mn`, `pixel_sat`, and `vib_w` unconditionally, regardless of whether `vibrance` (or `params.vibrance`) is zero. When vibrance is 0, `vib_w` is unused in the final scale (`vib * vib_w * 0.6` = 0), making min/max/saturation computation dead work. For a 20MP image that is 20M iterations of `r2.max(g2).max(b2)` + `r2.min(g2).min(b2)` + one divide and one clamp that could be skipped with a simple branch on `vib != 0.0`. Similarly, saturation applies a per-pixel luma computation every time even when `sat == 1.0` (no-op scale).
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 275-284 confirm: luma, raw_mx, mx, mn, pixel_sat, vib_w are all computed unconditionally for every pixel regardless of `vib` value. However, note that `sat` has a non-zero baseline (BASELINE_SAT=1.20), so the luma computation is always needed for saturation; only the vibrance-specific min/max/pixel_sat could be guarded. The finding is real but the description slightly overstates — luma cannot be skipped even when vib==0.

title: build_post_lut calls tone_curve 65536 times, each potentially calling powf up to 3 times, totalling up to ~196K powf calls per process() invocation
description: Inside `build_post_lut`, `tone_curve` is called for each of 65536 LUT entries. `tone_curve` conditionally calls `powf` for shadows gamma, highlights gamma (both guarded by `abs() > 1e-4`), and the sRGB EOTF (`linear_to_srgb` which calls `powf(1.0/2.4)` for non-tiny values). In the common case (shadows != 0, highlights != 0, most values > 0.0031308), all three fire: ~196K `powf` calls per build. `powf` in wasm32 is a software fallback (no native instruction), making it significantly more expensive than on native x86 with `vrsqrtss` or hardware approximations. A fixed-point polynomial approximation or a two-segment approximation for the sRGB EOTF would eliminate most of this cost.
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 102-161 confirm: `tone_curve` calls `y.powf(gamma)` (line 115), `inv.powf(gamma)` (line 123), and `linear_to_srgb` (line 139) which calls `v.powf(1.0/2.4)` (line 81) for values above 0.0031308. All three are called in the common case. `build_post_lut` calls `tone_curve` for all 65536 entries unconditionally.

title: LUTs in process() are pure functions of params and could be cached across apply_look calls when params are unchanged
description: The four LUTs (`pre_r`, `pre_g`, `pre_b`, `post`) are entirely determined by `PipelineParams`. On every slider drag event, `apply_look` is called with params that typically differ in only one field (e.g. contrast changes the post-LUT but not the pre-LUTs; WB changes only the pre-LUTs). Caching the LUTs on the JS side as `Uint16Array` / `Uint8Array` blobs and passing them in, or moving LUT construction into a separate WASM export (`build_luts(params) -> LutHandle`), would allow `apply_look` to skip LUT rebuild when the relevant params are unchanged. This is the highest-leverage single change for interactive responsiveness.
evidence: The LUTs are pure functions of PipelineParams fields (confirmed in pipeline.rs lines 241-252). No caching mechanism exists in the current code. The observation that only one LUT group changes per slider interaction is architecturally correct: pre-LUTs depend on wb/exposure/black/white/temp/tint; post-LUT depends on contrast/shadows/highlights/whites/blacks. The opportunity is real and high-value.

title: process() inner pixel loop is a good candidate for WASM SIMD via std::arch::wasm32
description: The per-pixel loop in `process` does a 3×3 matrix multiply, 3-component luma dot product, and per-channel scale — all branchy-free floating-point that maps well to `f32x4` WASM SIMD intrinsics. The LUT lookups (scatter/gather) cannot be vectorized, but the matrix and saturation stages before the lookup could process 4 pixels at once using `v128` instructions. Given the loop runs 20M times for a full-resolution render, even a partial vectorization of the matrix stage would be impactful. This requires `wasm32` target feature `simd128` and explicit intrinsics (auto-vectorization is unlikely due to the LUT gather at the end).
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 264-293 confirm the loop structure: 3×3 matrix multiply + luma dot product + min/max saturation per pixel, followed by LUT gather lookups. The matrix and saturation stages (lines 270-284) are indeed branchless float arithmetic suitable for f32x4 WASM SIMD. The LUT gather (lines 287-292) blocks full vectorization. This is a real opportunity with meaningful but bounded complexity.

title: rotate_180 copies pixels three bytes at a time instead of as a u32 or u24 word
description: The `rotate_180` function iterates columns and copies 3 bytes individually (`dst_row[c*3]`, `dst_row[c*3+1]`, `dst_row[c*3+2]`). A single `dst_row[c*3..c*3+3].copy_from_slice(&s_row[sc*3..sc*3+3])` would let LLVM emit a 3-byte or 4-byte load-store, and the reversed-column copy could also be split into a row-reversal using chunks of 3 bytes via a `rev()` chunk iterator. For a 20MP image rotated 180°, this is 60M byte copies. The current form is unlikely to auto-vectorize because of the non-contiguous scatter pattern (reversed column index).
evidence: pipeline.rs lines 400-415 match exactly. The three individual byte assignments per pixel (lines 410-412) are confirmed. The reversed column index `sc = w - 1 - c` does prevent straightforward auto-vectorization. `copy_from_slice` for 3-byte chunks would be cleaner, though actual speedup depends on whether LLVM can already merge the three adjacent stores.

title: Decompressor recomputes multiple raw[row*width + col ± 2] index expressions redundantly for the north-west predictor
description: In the main decompression loop, when `row >= 2 && col >= 2`, three reads occur: `out[row*width + col - 2]`, `out[(row-2)*width + col]`, and `out[(row-2)*width + col - 2]`. The multiplications `row*width` and `(row-2)*width` are recomputed from scratch each iteration rather than being maintained as incremented row-offset variables. LLVM's strength-reduction should handle this, but the branch structure (three separate `if row < 2 / col < 2` guards) adds branch cost that could be resolved by precomputing `row_base = row * width` and `prev_row_base = (row - 2) * width` outside the column loop. Low severity since decompression is a one-shot operation.
evidence: decompress.rs lines 61-82 match: `row * width` is recomputed in the col-loop body at lines 64, 66, 68, 69, 70, and `(row-2) * width` at lines 66, 69, 70. LLVM strength-reduction likely eliminates the multiply, but hoisting `row_base` and `prev_row_base` is still a valid micro-optimization. The one-shot nature of decompression correctly keeps this low severity.

title: Timing instrumentation covers only decompress/demosaic/tonemap/orient but not the two downscale_rgb16_impl calls or apply_unsharp_masks
description: The `now_ms()` timing in `process_orf` wraps decompress, demosaic, and tonemap+orient, but `downscale_rgb16_impl` (called twice, for lb and thumb) and `apply_unsharp_masks` (called before `process`) are not individually timed. Given that a 20MP → 1800px downscale with a box-filter over ~9 source pixels per output pixel is ~29M pixel reads, and unsharp masks dominate at ~480MB of allocation, profiling data for these stages would be valuable for optimization prioritization.
evidence: lib.rs confirms: `decompress_ms`, `demosaic_ms`, `tonemap_ms`, and `orient_ms` are measured (lines 223-313), but the two `downscale_rgb16_impl` calls (lines 272, 282) and the `apply_unsharp_masks` call (line 304) have no timing wrappers. The gap is exactly as described.

title: strip_offset + strip_byte_count can overflow usize on wasm32, defeating the bounds guard
description: On wasm32, usize is 32 bits. Both strip_offset and strip_byte_count are u32 values read from the attacker-controlled ORF file. Their sum on line 217 is computed as usize arithmetic, so if strip_offset = 0xFFFFFFF0 and strip_byte_count = 0x20, strip_end wraps to 0x10. The guard on line 218 (`strip_end > data.len()`) then passes for any file >= 16 bytes. The subsequent slice `&data[info.strip_offset as usize..strip_end]` has start > end and Rust panics (WASM trap), producing a reliable denial-of-service. No memory unsafety occurs because the safe-Rust slice panics, but an attacker can trap the WASM worker with a crafted header.
evidence: lib.rs lines 217-221 match exactly. Both `strip_offset` and `strip_byte_count` are `u32` fields (tiff.rs lines 19-20) cast to usize before addition, with no overflow check. On wasm32 (32-bit usize), the addition can wrap. The wrap causes `strip_end < strip_offset`, making the slice `start > end` which panics in safe Rust, causing a DoS trap. Fix: use `(info.strip_offset as usize).checked_add(info.strip_byte_count as usize)`.

title: get_unchecked in demosaic `at()` is safe for normal images but the assert does not prevent stride-overflow OOB on wasm32
description: The assertion `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)` (line 27) and the zero-dimension guard in lib.rs together mean `at()` is safe for well-formed inputs. However, on wasm32 where usize=u32, if an attacker supplies width and height both close to 65535, then `width * height` wraps during the assert (producing the same wrapped value that `decompress` used to allocate `out`), and the assert passes. Inside the demosaic loop, `row * width + col` also wraps identically, so `get_unchecked` aliases within the allocated buffer rather than escaping it — no true OOB in practice. The real residual risk is that the `unsafe` block provides no auditable proof of safety without studying all callers, and any future change that modifies allocation size without updating the assert would silently introduce UB. Severity is medium because current call chains are safe, but the unsafe is structurally fragile.
evidence: demosaic.rs lines 11-14 confirmed. The `assert_eq!(raw.len(), width * height)` at line 27 does overflow-match `decompress`'s `vec![0u16; width * height]`, so on wasm32 a wrapped allocation means the assert passes and `get_unchecked` aliasing stays within bounds. However, the caller (lib.rs line 259) only reaches demosaic after checking `width != 0 && height != 0` (line 108), but there is no cap on width/height values from the file, so the overflow scenario is real for crafted files. The finding is real but correctly self-assessed as 'no true OOB in current code' — the risk is future-regression fragility of the unsafe block.

title: as_ascii value_off + count addition can overflow u32, silently truncating parsed strings
description: In IfdEntry::as_ascii, the range `self.value_off as usize .. (self.value_off + self.count) as usize` computes the addition in u32 before casting. If value_off is 0xFFFFFF00 and count is 0x200, the sum wraps to 0xFF. The .get() call then returns None (start > end is caught), so the string is silently empty rather than causing a panic. While not a memory safety issue, this means crafted tag values can cause critical metadata (make/model/lens) to be silently lost, potentially bypassing downstream logic that relies on those fields. Should be computed as `(self.value_off as usize).saturating_add(self.count as usize)`.
code_excerpt:
let bytes = r
            .data
            .get(self.value_off as usize..(self.value_off + self.count) as usize)
            .unwrap_or(&[]);
        String::from_utf8_lossy(bytes)
evidence: tiff.rs lines 261-265 confirm: `(self.value_off + self.count)` is computed as u32 addition (both fields are u32) before casting to usize. If both are large and sum wraps, the resulting range has end < start and `.get()` returns `None`, silently yielding empty string. The fix is `(self.value_off as usize).saturating_add(self.count as usize)`. Real but not a memory-safety issue.

title: base_off as u32 + val sub-IFD offset arithmetic overflows u32 in MakerNote parsing
description: In parse_olympus_makernote, sub-IFD offsets for Equipment (0x2010), CameraSettings (0x2020), and ImageProcessing (0x2040) are computed as `base_off as u32 + val` before being passed to sub-IFD parsers. base_off is a usize cast to u32 (truncating on 64-bit), and val is attacker-controlled from the ORF. If the sum overflows u32, the resulting offset is passed to the sub-IFD parser which does a `p + 2 > r.data.len()` check, but with a wrapped (small) p value the check passes and the parser reads IFD entries from near offset 0 of the file. This causes wrong data to be parsed as WB/lens metadata — a data-confusion rather than memory-safety issue. Each sub-parser is otherwise bounds-checked.
evidence: tiff.rs lines 363, 367, 418 confirm: `base_off as u32 + val` is computed in u32 arithmetic (where `base_off: usize` is cast to u32 and `val: u32` is attacker-controlled). On wasm32 this is always 32-bit; on 64-bit hosts the cast truncates. The overflow wraps the offset to a small value, which passes the `p + 2 > r.data.len()` guard in sub-parsers (lines 446, 474, 493), causing reads from near file offset 0 — data confusion but no memory safety violation. Real finding.

title: downscale_rgb length check overflows usize on wasm32 for large attacker-supplied dimensions
description: The exported WASM function downscale_rgb accepts src_w and src_h from JavaScript without upper-bound validation. On wasm32, `sw * sh * 3` can overflow usize when dimensions are large (e.g. src_w=50000, src_h=50000: 50000*50000*3 = 7.5B > 4GB). The overflow makes the length check on line 367 compare against a wrapped value, allowing it to pass with a mismatched src buffer. The inner loop then accesses `src[(y * sw + x) * 3 + 2]`, which Rust's bounds checker traps — producing a WASM panic rather than memory corruption, but enabling reliable DoS from JS. A similar overflow exists in the output allocation `dw * dh * 3`.
evidence: lib.rs lines 366-372 confirm: `sw * sh * 3` is usize multiplication with no overflow check on line 367, and `dw * dh * 3` similarly on line 372. On wasm32, both can wrap for large JS-supplied dimensions. The length check uses the wrapped value, potentially passing incorrectly; subsequent inner-loop accesses will panic via Rust's bounds checker causing a WASM trap / DoS. The `downscale_rgb16_impl` internal function (line 144) has the same issue but is only called with internally-computed dimensions.
